exemplar.title: How does the transmission of UV light through coloured light filters affect the concentration of linalool during the storage of extracted Basil oil, dissolved in ethanol, as measured by Gas Chromatography Mass Spectrometry?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the transmission of UV light through coloured light filters affect the concentration of linalool during the storage of extracted Basil oil, dissolved in ethanol, as measured by Gas Chromatography Mass Spectrometry?

strengths: Clear and sustained communication of the investigationâs purpose, with accurate description of the topic and relevant chemical context
Explicitly phrased research question focusing on a single analyte and variable
Comprehensive step-by-step procedural description supporting replicability
weaknesses: Some digressions and redundancy dilute focus and precision
Conceptual misalignment between the chosen visible-light metric and the UV absorption relevant to linalool weakens coherence
Methodological controls (e.g., dark control, replication, blank runs) are incomplete or under-specified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates an adequate but not fully effective focus and method. The research question is clear and the methodology is generally replicable, but conceptual misalignment and incomplete experimental controls limit the sharpness and robustness of the approach. This performance aligns with the mid-band descriptor for Focus and Method, warranting a score of 3 out of 6.

strengths: Accurate and consistent use of subject-specific terminology (e.g., photodegradation, retention time, %RSD)
Clear and coherent knowledge of photochemical processes and GC-MS operation
Relevant application of source material to explain photodegradation pathways and justify instrumentation
weaknesses: Occasional mixing of visible-light and UV terminology reduces precision
Application of Y-transmission data is mis-aligned with UV attenuation without quantitative support
Key quantitative benchmarks and detailed instrument parameters (e.g., ionization mode) are under-developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows clear and coherent subject knowledge with mostly appropriate use of terminology and relevant application of sources. Occasional imprecision and under-developed quantitative integration prevent it from reaching the highest band. This warrants a score of 4 out of 6 in Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: Good research effort with primary GC-MS data and a highly linear calibration curve (RÂ² = 0.999)
Data processing includes concentration calculations, uncertainty tabulation, and clear graphical presentation
Thoughtful critical reflection on methodological limitations and sensible improvement proposals
weaknesses: Statistical treatment is limited (no regression or significance testing, error bars omitted)
Analysis remains largely descriptive with limited quantitative exploration of alternative explanations
Evaluations are qualitative and lack quantification of error magnitude or impact
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation achieves good research quality and clear analysis with well-supported conclusions, complemented by thoughtful evaluation of limitations. However, the absence of robust statistical treatment and quantitative critical evaluation limits it to the mid-upper range of the Critical Thinking criterion. A score of 9 out of 12 accurately reflects this performance.

strengths: Clearly appropriate structure following IB conventions with logical sequencing of sections
Well-ordered tables, figures, and bibliography enable reader navigation
Formal requirements such as title, word count, and page numbering are met
weaknesses: Occasional redundancy in captions and table entries disrupts flow
Some figures are low resolution or repetitive, and a few tables exceed width recommendations
Accessibility considerations (e.g., patterning for color-blind readers) are not fully addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs organization and layout are generally clear and meet all major IB presentation conventions, despite minor lapses in figure quality and redundancy. This level of performance corresponds to a score of 3 out of 4 in Presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay follows IB EE conventions: title page, abstract/word count, contents, sequential sections (introduction, methodology, results, evaluation, conclusion, references, appendices).  Headings, tables and figures are logically ordered, allowing the reader to navigate the argument.  Minor issuesâoccasional redundancy, some lengthy captions, and an over-long background sectionâmoderately detract from overall flow, but structure remains clearly appropriate.

The student states a clear and specific research focus (quantifying the influence of UV-filtered visible light on linalool concentration in basil oil) and provides an extended background section that explains why the topic is relevant to essential-oil stability and commercial storage practice. Key chemical concepts (photodegradation, photo-oxidation, isomerisation) are introduced with appropriate citation and the link to coloured filters/bottle storage is articulated. Occasional digressions (e.g. therapeutic uses of essential oils) and some redundancy mean the communication is more adequate than exemplary, but overall purpose and focus are evident and sustained.

Data processing includes calibration, conversion of peak areas to concentrations, tabulation of uncertainties and bar-chart visualisations.  However, statistical treatment is limited: no regression or correlation analysis of filter transmission versus linalool loss, no significance testing, and partial propagation of error (uncertainties absent from graphs).  Interpretation of results remains largely descriptive, with limited exploration of alternative explanations beyond acknowledging flaws.  The analysis is clear but only moderately developed, meriting the lower end of the 4â6 band.

The investigation gathers primary experimental data via GC-MS and complements it with pertinent secondary studies.  The calibration curve (RÂ² = 0.999) and quantified concentrations indicate a sound approach to data generation.  Nevertheless, limited replication (some colours have only one or two valid runs), unverified UV intensities and the acknowledged inability to answer the RQ constrain overall relevance and completeness.  The research effort is solid but not consistently aligned with the stated question, situating it within the âgood/mostly relevantâ portion of the 4â6 band.

The student demonstrates clear knowledge of photochemical processes (photodegradation, photo-oxidation), GC-MS operation and essential-oil chemistry, employing appropriate chemical explanations (e.g., retention time, m/z, linalool structure).  The discussion is generally coherent, though some explanatory passages are descriptive rather than analytical.  Occasional misconceptions (equating visible-light Y-transmission with UV transmittance) prevent the work from reaching the highest level of sophisticated, consistently integrated understanding.

Subject-specific terminologyâphotodegradation, hydroperoxide, retention time, calibration curve, coefficient of determination (RÂ²), %RSDâis used accurately and in context throughout most of the essay.  Occasional lapses in precision (e.g., mixing visible and UV terminology) occur but do not impede comprehension.  Chemical nomenclature and units are generally correct, supporting a mostly consistent technical register appropriate to IB Chemistry.

A full, step-by-step description of apparatus, quantities, uncertainties and procedures (extraction, UV exposure, GC-MS calibration and analysis) is presented, demonstrating that the work could be broadly replicated.  The student justifies some choices (e.g., ethanol as solvent, 40 cm lamp distance) and includes three pilot studies.  Nevertheless, methodological limitations acknowledged in the evaluation â such as lack of repeat trials, uncontrolled UV intensity, unverified filter UV transmittance and partial data loss through evaporation â reveal that selection and control of methods were only partly effective.  Hence the methodology is mostly complete but not fully appropriate or optimally chosen.

Tables and figures are numbered, titled and generally referenced in the text; units and uncertainties are usually indicated; the bibliography follows a consistent citation style.  Page numbers, word count and title are present, fulfilling formal requirements.  Some figures are low resolution or contain superfluous repetition, and a few tables exceed recommended width, but these lapses are minor.  Overall, layout considerations are met in a clear though not flawless manner.

The student reflects critically on methodological weaknesses, identifies systematic and random errors, and proposes sensible improvements (e.g., verifying UV transmittance, sealing containers, increasing repeats).  The conclusion recognises that the RQ is not fully answered and links findings to literature.  Nonetheless, evaluations are primarily qualitative; they rarely quantify error magnitude or predict the impact of proposed changes, and some discussion (e.g., speculation about green filters) lacks firm evidential support.  Consequently the work meets the descriptors for good but not excellent critical evaluation.

The literature cited is generally relevant (photochemistry of linalool, EO storage studies, GC-MS principles).  Key sources are used to explain photodegradation pathways and justify instrument choice.  However, the application of some data is superficial; for instance, the percentage Y-transmission values (visible light) are treated as if they describe UV attenuation without supporting evidence.  Quantitative benchmarks from the literature for expected degradation rates are not integrated into the analysis.  Overall, source use is mostly relevant but occasionally mis-aligned or under-developed.

The research question is explicitly worded, focused on one analyte (linalool) and one manipulation (UV transmission through coloured filters).  It is placed early in the report and referred to in subsequent sections.  However, the student later concedes that the chosen light-transmission metric (Y-value for visible light) does not in fact match the UV range relevant to linalool absorption, indicating incomplete conceptual alignment between the question and the executed investigation.  This limits the sharpness of focus and weakens the coherence of later discussion, keeping the strand in the mid band rather than the highest.

annotations.comment: The independent and controlled variable tables clearly identify and justify experimental controls, supporting replicability.
A well-structured table of contents provides clear navigation through the sections, supporting reader orientation.
The chemical structure of linalool is clearly depicted with labelled functional groups, reinforcing conceptual understanding of its photochemistry.
The molecular formula is presented inline rather than in LaTeX; consider using $$C_{10}H_{18}O$$ to maintain consistency with other equations.
The discussion of photodegradation and photooxidation would benefit from quantitative rate data or literature kinetics to deepen analytical rigor.
Y-transmittance values pertain to visible light but are used to infer UV attenuation; direct measurement of UV transmission is needed to align data with the research question.
The GC-MS methodology lacks detail on ionization mode or selected ion monitoring parameters; include these to demonstrate full command of instrumentation.
The discussion of Y-value terminology would benefit from a concise technical definition linking CIE Y to spectral transmittance rather than general human perception.
Abbreviation of GC-MS is inconsistent; adopt a single format (e.g., GC-MS) throughout to maintain technical coherence.
Separate chromatograms for each replicate increase redundancy; consider combining into a multi-panel figure to conserve space and enhance comparison.
Green-filter chromatograms lack annotation of the linalool peak on the plot; marking retention time directly would improve data interpretation.
Error propagation is described textually but not visualized; including error bars on processed-data figures would illustrate uncertainty more effectively.
Table 6.2.1 lists uncertainties but does not explain propagation methods; a brief methodological note would aid reader understanding of statistical treatment.
Bar chart colours alone convey data distinctions; add patterns or direct labels for color-blind accessibility and clarity.
Appendix statement is well documented but sits after the bibliography; IB guidelines recommend appendices follow references, so reordering is suggested.
The inclusion of a clear word count at the start satisfies IB formatting requirements and ensures compliance with the word limit.
A precise title and research question are stated early in the report, enhancing clarity and meeting formal report conventions.
The research question is explicitly phrased and focuses on a single analyte and variable, establishing clear purpose and direction.
The table of contents duplicates entries and ranges across two pages, which could be consolidated to improve readability and reduce redundancy.

The narrative around adapting the research question after methodology development shows flexibility but indicates an initial misalignment; ensure future questions align conceptually with available experimental setups.
The background cites linalool absorption below 295 nm but could be strengthened by integrating specific absorption spectra to justify the UV range requirement quantitatively.
Table 2.1 repeats the header mid-table which interrupts the data flow; consider restructuring or splitting into separate tables for clarity.
A dark control (no light) is not explicitly included; consider adding a fully blocked sample to quantify baseline linalool stability.
Pilot smell tests provide qualitative insight but are subjective; incorporating preliminary GC-MS measurements in pilot studies would yield more robust data.
Trial modifications address extract solubility but omit plans for replication; incorporate repeated independent experiments to reduce random error.
Photograph of distillation setup is useful but would be clearer as a schematic diagram with uniform line weights and labels.
The UV lamp setup image lacks scale or dimension annotations; adding a schematic with annotated distances improves methodological transparency.
Sample preparation omits description of blank and solvent runs to account for background peaks; include blank analyses as standard practice.
The calibration curve lacks the reported RÂ² value on the plot, which is essential for evaluating calibration reliability.
Mechanistic schemes of photooxidation are detailed but lack kinetic or yield data; including such quantitative information would strengthen analysis depth.
The comprehensive illustration of oxidation products contextualises potential photodegradation pathways, demonstrating effective use of source material.
Clear-filter chromatograms show a dominant non-linalool peak; discuss potential interferences and implement purity checks or use selective ion monitoring.
Limitations are acknowledged but lack quantification of impact magnitude; estimating effect sizes would enhance critical evaluation.
Errors are classified as random or systematic, but some (e.g., temperature variation) may be systematic; revising classifications would improve error analysis accuracy.
